items.question_text: Under 13-1-6, when automated coordination with a facility is not available, manual coordination status may be annotated using the Coordination Menu or a _____.
items.explanation: 13-1-6(a) provides two methods for annotating manual coordination: the Coordination Menu or a flight progress strip. Both must be used in accordance with facility directives.

choices.text: flight progress strip

items.question_text: Under 13-1-6, which facilities are given as examples of where automated coordination may not be available?
items.explanation: 13-1-6(a) gives international facilities and VFR towers as examples. En route ARTCCs, TRACONs, and military radar units typically have automated coordination through ERAM.

choices.text: An international facility or a VFR tower.
An en route ARTCC or a consolidated TRACON.
A military radar approach control or an AFSS.
Any facility operating under a different FAA order than JO 7110.65.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-6(b), after the Coordination Menu is used and the flight plan is subsequently changed, the yellow coding on the Coordination Indicator must be removed:
items.explanation: 13-1-6(b) states the yellow coding is removed 'after any appropriate action has been taken.' It is not removed immediately upon the change, nor is it left indefinitely. The action must be completed first.

choices.text: Immediately when the flight plan change is entered, before any follow-up action is taken.
Only after the aircraft has been handed off and is no longer in the sector.
After any appropriate action has been taken.
Never. The coordination indicator remains as a permanent record of the prior coordination event.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-7, list the five approved methods for annotating holding instructions for flights in hold.
items.explanation: 13-1-7 names five methods: Hold View, Hold Data Menu, hold message, flight progress strip, or facility-approved worksheet. Each must be used in accordance with facility directives.

choices.text: Hold View, Hold Data Menu, hold message, flight progress strip, or facility-approved worksheet

items.question_text: Under 13-1-7, holding instruction annotation must be performed in accordance with what?
items.explanation: The paragraph concludes with 'in accordance with facility directives.' The facility determines which approved method is specified for use.

choices.text: Facility directives.
The pilot's preference as stated on initial contact.
The controller's personal preference, since all five methods are equally valid.
The TMU's daily shift log requirements only.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-8, what must be done when a verbal point out has been approved?
items.explanation: 13-1-8(b) requires removing the yellow color coding on the ACL when a verbal point out has been approved. This is a specific ACL action distinct from general highlighting or Free Text entries.

choices.text: Enter the point out approval notation in the Free Text Area of the ACL.
Highlight the entry on the ACL to indicate the point out has been approved.
Remove the yellow color coding on the ACL.
Transfer the point out coding to the receiving sector's display list.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-8, which of the following is an authorized source for abbreviations used in the ACL or DL Free Text Area?
items.explanation: 13-1-8(c) lists five authorized sources: TBL 13-1-1 clearance abbreviations, TBL 13-1-2 miscellaneous abbreviations, TBL 13-1-3 EDST equivalents, plain language markings, and locally approved abbreviations. Pilot shorthand is not authorized.

choices.text: Clearance abbreviations authorized in TBL 13-1-1.
Any abbreviation commonly used by pilots in their filed remarks.
Personal shorthand developed by the controller, as long as the next sector can guess the meaning.
ICAO flight plan abbreviations from Doc 4444, which always take precedence over local ones.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-8 NOTE, information entered into the Free Text Area does not pass on handoff. What must be done if that information needs to reach the receiving controller?
items.explanation: The note states that Free Text Area information does not pass on handoff and, if necessary, must be coordinated. The controller cannot rely on Free Text to convey information to the next sector.

choices.text: it must be coordinated

items.question_text: Under 13-1-8, control information entered in the Free Text Area must be used for what purpose?
items.explanation: 13-1-8(e) states control information entered in the Free Text Area must be used for reference purposes only. It is not a substitute for official coordination, a permanent record, or handoff data.

choices.text: As an official coordination record that replaces verbal briefing on handoff.
As a primary clearance delivery method to the pilot via ACARS.
For reference purposes only.
As a permanent flight record that remains in the EAS archive.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-9, the IAFDOF feature must be used in automatic mode. What must remain deselected to maintain this mode?
items.explanation: 13-1-9(a) states IAFDOF Manual must remain deselected unless otherwise authorized in a facility directive. Automatic mode is the default; manual mode is the exception.

choices.text: IAFDOF Manual

items.question_text: Under 13-1-9, which coding must be acknowledged on the DL by removing the coding after issuance to the pilot or other control facility?
items.explanation: 13-1-9(d) specifically states EDCT coding is acknowledged on the DL. IAFDOF and UTM are on the ACL (b, c). ERT and RAN coding is not acknowledged on the DL.

choices.text: EDCT coding.
IAFDOF coding.
UTM coding.
ERT coding.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-9, must ERT coding be sent or acknowledged by a sector that does not have track control for the flight?
items.explanation: 13-1-9(f) says do not send/acknowledge ERT coding unless the sector has track control for the flight or it has been otherwise coordinated. The first sector displaying ERT handles it before handoff.

choices.text: No. ERT coding must not be sent or acknowledged unless the sector has track control or it has been otherwise coordinated.
Yes. Any sector that can see ERT coding is authorized to send or acknowledge it immediately.
Yes, but only if the ERT coding has been present on the display for more than two scan cycles.
No, but only if the sector is the first to display the ERT coding after the flight plan was originally filed.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-9, Route Action Notifications (RAN) must be amended at the first control position that displays the RAN unless _____ or as specified in appropriate facility directives.
items.explanation: 13-1-9(g) requires RAN amendment at the first control position displaying it, with two exceptions: verbal coordination or facility directive provisions. The same track control or coordination requirement applies before removing RAN coding.

choices.text: verbally coordinated

items.question_text: Under 13-1-10, list the data elements used to model an individual aircraft's trajectory.
items.explanation: 13-1-10 note 2 lists four elements: route of flight, assigned and interim altitudes, application/removal of adapted restriction for that flight, and aircraft type. These are the data that must be kept current for accurate conflict probe.

choices.text: route of flight, assigned and interim altitudes, application/removal of an adapted restriction, and aircraft type

items.question_text: Under 13-1-10, what is the consequence of not keeping trajectory-model data current?
items.explanation: 13-1-10 note 1 warns that stale data can make aircraft entries and notification of probe results misleading for surrounding sectors and facilities as well as the subject sector. The impact extends beyond the current sector.

choices.text: The automation automatically reverts to the filed flight plan and ignores any amendments until the data is updated.
Aircraft entries and probe result notifications for surrounding sectors and facilities, as well as the subject sector, may be misleading.
EDST disables conflict probe for the affected aircraft and issues a system alert to the operations supervisor.
Only the aircraft list display is affected; probe accuracy continues unaffected because it relies only on radar track data.

items.question_text: Under 13-1-10, when may an exception to the requirement to enter or update interim altitudes be authorized?
items.explanation: 13-1-10(b) provides an exception: 'for certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive.' This is not a blanket exception and not at the discretion of the individual controller.

choices.text: For certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive.
At any time the controller determines the interim altitude is obvious from the flight's radar track.
For all sectors at facilities below FL180, where interim altitudes are never operationally significant.
Whenever the sector is too busy to make non-essential data entries.

items.situation: You must coordinate a flight with a VFR tower that has no automated coordination capability. Your team uses the Coordination Menu to annotate the manual coordination. Later, the flight's route is amended. A trainee wants to leave the old yellow Coordination Indicator in place, saying it provides useful history for the next controller.
items.question_text: How should the Coordination Indicator be managed after the flight plan is changed?

choices.text: Leave the yellow coding permanently because it documents the prior manual coordination that occurred.
Remove the yellow coding immediately when the change is entered to keep the display clean.
Remove the yellow coding from the Coordination Indicator after any appropriate action has been taken regarding the flight plan change.
Change the yellow coding to a different color to indicate the coordination has been superseded.

items.explanation: 13-1-6(B) requires removing the yellow coding after any appropriate action has been taken when the flight plan is subsequently changed. The coding should not remain as history, nor be removed before action is complete.
items.para_context: MANUAL COORDINATION AND THE COORDINATION MENU

items.question_text: A flight is holding at an en route fix. The controller uses Hold View to annotate the holding instructions. Under 13-1-7, is this sufficient by itself?

choices.text: No. Hold View must always be supplemented with a facility-approved worksheet.
Yes, if used in accordance with facility directives. Hold View is one of the five approved annotation methods listed in 13-1-7.
No. Only a flight progress strip satisfies the holding annotation requirement.
Yes, but only if the Hold Data Menu is also used for the same holding aircraft.

items.explanation: 13-1-7 Lists Hold View as one of five approved methods, each of which is valid when used in accordance with facility directives. No single method requires supplementation by another.

items.situation: A controller enters control information in the ACL Free Text Area using locally approved abbreviations, then closes the Free Text Area to keep the display tidy. During a subsequent handoff, the next controller cannot see the information that was entered. The first controller says the information should have passed automatically.
items.question_text: What two rules were violated regarding Free Text Area management?

choices.text: The Free Text Area must remain open and visible, and information in it does not pass on handoff and must be coordinated if necessary. The controller violated both by closing the area and not coordinating the information.
Only the local abbreviation rule was violated. The Free Text Area may be closed when display space is limited, and information always passes on handoff.
No rules were violated. Free Text is for reference only and closing it is standard practice during busy operations.
The controller should have used plain language instead of abbreviations, but closing the area and not coordinating are both standard practice.

items.explanation: 13-1-8(D) requires the Free Text Area to remain open and visible. The note to 13-1-8 states Free Text information does not pass on handoff and must be coordinated if necessary. Both rules were violated.

items.question_text: A controller has just completed a verbal point out to an adjacent sector and received approval. What specific ACL action is required under 13-1-8?

choices.text: Remove the yellow color coding on the ACL.
Apply a highlight to the aircraft entry on the ACL.
Enter the point out approval time in the Free Text Area.
Transfer the point out coding to a flight progress strip for the receiving sector.

items.explanation: 13-1-8(B) is specific: when a verbal point out has been approved, remove the yellow color coding on the ACL. This acknowledges completion of the point out coordination on the display.

items.situation: An IAFDOF alert is displayed, an EDCT has been issued to the pilot, and a RAN warning appears for a route processing error on an aircraft your sector has track control for. A controller wants to acknowledge all three codings immediately to clean up the display before taking any action.
items.question_text: When may these codings be acknowledged?

choices.text: Each coding must be acknowledged only after the appropriate action has been completed. IAFDOF, UTM, and EDCT coding removal before action is completed violates 13-1-9(e).
All three may be acknowledged immediately to reduce display clutter. The actions can be completed afterward without violating any requirement.
EDCT may be acknowledged immediately because it was already issued, but IAFDOF and RAN must wait for action completion.
IAFDOF and RAN may be acknowledged immediately, but EDCT requires the pilot to verbally confirm receipt before removal.

items.explanation: 13-1-9(E) is explicit: IAFDOF, UTM, or EDCT coding must be acknowledged only after the appropriate action has been completed. Acknowledging before action completion is a violation regardless of workload.

items.question_text: The first sector displaying ERT coding must issue and send/acknowledge the route. When must this be accomplished relative to handoff?

choices.text: Prior to initiating a hand-off, unless verbally coordinated or as specified in appropriate facility directives.
After the hand-off has been initiated and accepted by the receiving sector.
At any time before the aircraft physically crosses the sector boundary.
Only after the receiving sector specifically requests the ERT route information.

items.explanation: 13-1-9(F) explicitly states the first sector displaying ERT must issue and send/acknowledge the route PRIOR TO initiating a hand-off, unless verbally coordinated or otherwise specified in facility directives.

items.situation: Your sector team has been busy and an aircraft's interim altitude change has not been entered in the automation. The aircraft is level at an intermediate altitude waiting for further climb. Another controller says the entry can wait because the aircraft is showing level on radar, and the next sector will enter it on handoff. The sector has no facility directive authorizing an exception for interim altitudes.
items.question_text: Must the interim altitude be entered now?

choices.text: Yes. The sector team must perform automation entries in a timely manner, and conflict probe accuracy in assigning alert notification is dependent upon entry/update of a flight's interim altitude. The aircraft being level on radar does not eliminate this requirement.
No. The aircraft is level on radar, so the automation will self-correct the trajectory model based on track data alone. The entry can wait.
No. Interim altitude updates are always optional unless the aircraft is in a non-radar environment.
Yes, but only because the sector has no facility directive exception. If the sector were busy enough, the entry could be skipped at the controller's discretion.

items.explanation: 13-1-10 Requires timely automation entries, specifically notes conflict probe alert accuracy depends on interim altitude entry/update, and provides an exception only if explicitly defined in a facility directive — which this sector does not have.
